sources_audited.note: The exact Census URL returned an HTTP 403 anti-bot page, so the six-digit definition and its use as support for the narrowed external-customer lens could not be independently confirmed in this audit.
The exact BLS URL returned an Access Denied page, so the stated 2023 occupation shares could not be independently confirmed in this audit.

The PolyU page supports real-time AI fabric-defect inspection, roughly 40 defect types, and a reported detection rate above 90%; it describes weaving-machine use and does not establish coated-fabric labor savings or five-year implementation.

The report supports the cited broad-manufacturing figures: 72% current vision-system use, 18% formal manufacturing-operations AI guidance, 65% management trust/ability barriers, 82% skills barriers, and 78% connectivity/integration/legacy-system barriers.

EPA supports inclusion of fabric coating and laminating in textile-mill processes, PFAS performance uses and application routes, sparse monitoring, and observed PFAS in limited textile-mill effluent samples; it does not directly measure acquisition transferability, implementation rates, or sale completion.

Gallup supports the 52.3% owner-age statistic, the 22% five-year sell/transfer plan among employer-business owners, and the disclosed survey design; it measures cross-industry intentions rather than completed coating-mill control transfers.

The exact FRED series page could not be retrieved after repeated reopening attempts, so the stated annual index values were not independently confirmed; even if confirmed, the series would not support the separate pricing-retention claims for q.

SelectUSA supports $25.3 billion of 2023 textile-mill shipments and broad specialty/industrial applications including automotive, insulation, filters, hoses, tires, and composites, but it is not specific to coated fabrics and does not support unit pricing, qualification frictions, or retained AI benefit.

The Trade.gov page supports Berry Amendment restrictions covering synthetic and coated synthetic fabric, tents, tarpaulins, covers, canvas products, and clothing materials, subject to listed exceptions; it does not quantify lens-firm defense exposure or demand growth.

caveats: S1, S2, and S7 could not be substantively reopened from their exact URLs, so the NAICS definition, occupation shares, and annual output values were not independently verified in this review.

The q rationale about physical-unit pricing, customer qualification, rebids, and five-year benefit retention is not supported by S7 or S8; q is honestly labeled ESTIMATE, and correcting or downgrading this support would not change the STRUCTURAL_OUT base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

S5 establishes PFAS use, sparse monitoring, and limited wastewater detections, but the effects on acquisition transferability, sale completion, and implementation are judgmental extensions rather than directly observed evidence.
S8 describes the broader textile sector and specialty-fabric applications, not NAICS 313320 demand or the current customer baskets of external-customer coating mills.
S9 establishes a domestic-source rule with exceptions but does not show how many screened firms serve covered Defense Department programs or how much demand the rule anchors.
The disclosed lens is coherent but materially narrower than the six-digit industry, and no source quantifies the share of firms or revenue arising from repeat external-customer coating programs.
S3 demonstrates technical inspection feasibility on woven-fabric production but does not establish coated-fabric applicability, realized labor substitution, false-reject performance, or payback.
S4 is a broad-manufacturing survey whose respondents may be larger and more digitally mature than lower-middle-market fabric coaters; the record discloses this bridge and uncertainty.
summary: Mechanics pass. The record is useful and internally coherent, with honest ESTIMATE and PROXY labels, disclosed lens narrowing, broad ranges, and a conclusion that follows from a small transferable-firm pool and weak labor-opportunity factor. Several exact source pages were not reopenable, and the reachable sources often support only broad textile, manufacturing, environmental, or defense context rather than coating-specific pricing, transferability, or five-year implementation. Those weaknesses warrant caveats but do not pass the methodology's materiality test: the base tier is already STRUCTURAL_OUT and estimate-driven readiness already fixes the action at VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS.
